text: The surgeon uses laparoscopic instruments to hold the bladder, revealing the rectum below. Behind the rectum, a large cystic structure occupies the pelvic cavity, identified as the cystic component of the teratoma. The solid component extends from the sacrum to the perineum, between the coccyx and anal region. Dissection begins to separate the rectum from the cystic teratoma while preserving rectal vascularity.

The surgeon aspirates fluid from the large cystic component of the teratoma and sends it for cytology. The rectum is hitched to the anterior abdominal wall to free both hands for dissection. The surgeon carefully dissects between the rectum and the cyst, identifying and maintaining an avascular plane to preserve rectal vascularity. The dissection proceeds towards the fundus of the cyst, ensuring minimal bleeding by adhering to the mesorectal plane.

The laparoscopic view provides clear visualization of the surgical planes, allowing for atraumatic dissection. The surgeon has separated the fundus of the cyst and completed most of the lateral dissections up to the ureter level. The focus is now on moving downwards posteriorly, taking care to avoid damaging the pre-sacral plexus of veins and the parasympathetic outflow from S3 to S4.

The surgeon carefully defines the dissection planes around the cyst, using a monopolar cautery to perform lateral dissection. The final strands are being separated, and the surgeon aspirates additional fluid from the cyst. A Covedian ligature vessel sealing device is then used to separate the cyst from the pre-sacral space, ensuring hemostasis and minimizing nerve damage.

The surgeon carefully dissects the tumor in the pre-sacral space, removing adhesion bands while staying close to the mass to avoid deeper dissection into the sacrum. The dissection progresses laterally near the ischiopubic ramus, with the cyst hitched to the anterior abdominal wall. Residual bands are loosened, ensuring the cyst wall is free from intrapelvic structures for eventual delivery from the perineum.

The surgeon completes the abdominal dissection, ensuring hemostasis and verifying the integrity of the rectum and uterus. The patient is repositioned to prone, and a central incision is made from S-V down to the cyst. The solid component of the teratoma is dissected, taking care not to damage the rectum. The cyst is delivered out, followed by coccygectomy to reduce recurrence risk. The final specimen, approximately twenty centimeters in length, is removed from the perineum.
